title: Streams API
objectives: Understand streams through a real-life analogy
Build pipelines with intermediate + terminal operations
Grasp laziness and short-circuiting — the source of stream speed

sections.markdown:
**Imagine a factory assembly line.**

- Bags of raw parts arrive at one end (the **source** — a collection, file, or generator).
- The line has **stations** — one filters out bad parts, one polishes them, one sorts by size, one packages. That's your **intermediate operations** (`filter`, `map`, `sorted`).
- At the far end, someone counts finished packages or ships them out. That's your **terminal operation** (`count`, `collect`, `forEach`).

Crucially: **nothing actually moves along the line until the terminal station asks for output.** The stations are lazy — they wait until someone at the end says 'go.' That's why streams can be huge (or infinite) without blowing up memory.

**A stream is not a data structure.** It's a *description* of a computation over data. The data doesn't sit in the stream — it flows through it, item by item, when demanded.

**The Streams API (Java 8+)** processes sequences of elements declaratively through a **pipeline** of operations — `filter`, `map`, `reduce`, `collect` — instead of imperative loops. Streams are lazy, composable, and can transparently go parallel.
## The anatomy of a pipeline

rows: filter, map, sorted, distinct, limit, peek, flatMap
Return a new Stream. Don't execute anything.
collect, forEach, count, reduce, min/max, findFirst, toArray, allMatch/anyMatch
Trigger the pipeline. Return a result or side effect.
findFirst, findAny, anyMatch, allMatch, noneMatch, limit
Can stop early once they have an answer.

sections.markdown: **Streams are LAZY. That's the whole point.** Chaining 5 intermediate operations doesn't do 5 passes over your data — it does *one* pass, applying each operation to each element. Short-circuit terminals like `findFirst` stop at the first match. On an infinite stream, `.limit(10).forEach(println)` prints 10 things and stops — the infinity is only theoretical.

## Collectors — the terminal Swiss Army knife

`Collectors` is where streams get truly expressive. Every real backend uses these one-liners hundreds of times.
## A complete sample program — see everything

## Parallel streams — when they actually help

`.parallel()` on any stream splits the work across CPU cores using the common `ForkJoinPool`. Sounds like a free lunch — it isn't.

rows: I/O-bound (use CompletableFuture or virtual threads instead)

sections.markdown: ## Common beginner mistakes

**Mistake 1: Reusing a stream after a terminal operation.**

```java
Stream<Integer> s = list.stream();
s.count();          // terminal — stream is now consumed
s.forEach(...);     // IllegalStateException: stream has already been operated upon or closed
```

Streams are single-use. Create a new one from the source if you need to iterate twice.

**Mistake 2: `parallel()` on everything.**

Parallel streams have real overhead — splitting work, coordinating threads, merging results. On small data or I/O-bound work, they're slower. Benchmark before parallelizing.

**Mistake 3: Side effects inside stream operations.**

```java
List<Integer> results = new ArrayList<>();
numbers.stream().parallel().forEach(n -> results.add(n * 2));   // RACE!
```

Use `.collect(toList())` — collectors handle parallelism correctly. Never mutate shared state from a stream.
## Best practices
**1. Chain operations declaratively — no intermediate variables.** `stream().filter().map().collect()` reads top to bottom.
**2. Prefer `toList()` (Java 16+) over `Collectors.toList()`** — returns unmodifiable, slightly faster.
**3. Use `mapToInt` / `mapToLong` for numeric reductions.** Avoid autoboxing overhead. `stream().mapToInt(Person::salary).sum()`.
**4. Use `groupingBy` + `counting` / `averaging` / `mapping` for one-line analytics.** These downstream collectors are extremely powerful.
**5. Reach for parallel only when you have measured a benefit.**
## Practice programs

**Practice 1 (Easy) — Sum even numbers.** Use `IntStream.filter(n -> n % 2 == 0).sum()` on a list of 1–100.

**Why:** Baseline stream fluency.

**Practice 2 (Medium) — Group and rank.** Given a list of orders (customer, total), print top 3 customers by total spend using `groupingBy` + `summingDouble` + `.entrySet().stream().sorted().limit(3)`.

**Why:** The single most common backend query in stream form.

**Practice 3 (Hard) — Word frequency.** Read a text file, split on whitespace, count occurrences of each word, print the top 10.

**Why:** Classic text-analysis pattern; combines flatMap + groupingBy + sorted.

**Practice 4 (Expert) — Custom Collector.** Write a `Collector` that computes `(min, max, avg)` in one pass instead of three separate stream operations.

**Why:** Once you can write your own Collector, streams stop feeling magical.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What are intermediate and terminal operations, and why does it matter?**

**A:** **Intermediate operations** (`filter`, `map`, `sorted`, `distinct`, `limit`, `flatMap`, `peek`) return a new Stream and are **lazy** — they don't execute anything, they just describe what to do. **Terminal operations** (`collect`, `forEach`, `count`, `reduce`, `min/max`, `findFirst`, `toArray`, `anyMatch`) are **eager** — they trigger the pipeline. Why it matters: the pipeline executes only *once*, on demand, and can **short-circuit** with terminals like `findFirst` — no wasted work. Chaining 5 intermediate operations is still a single pass over the data, not five. This laziness is what makes streams both expressive and fast.

**Q2: Why are streams lazy, and what does that enable?**

**A:** Laziness — no work happens until a terminal operation is invoked — enables three superpowers. **(1) Operation fusion:** the pipeline runs element-by-element in a single pass, applying all intermediate ops to each element before moving to the next. No intermediate lists get created. **(2) Short-circuiting:** `findFirst`, `anyMatch`, and `limit` can stop the pipeline the moment they have their answer — no scanning the rest of the data. **(3) Infinite streams:** `Stream.iterate` and `Stream.generate` produce infinite sequences that are safe because you only consume as much as a terminal like `limit(10)` needs. If streams were eager, none of this would be possible.

**Q3: What's the difference between `map` and `flatMap`?**

**A:** **`map(fn)`** applies `fn` to each element and produces one output per input. `Stream<String>` → `Stream<Integer>` via `map(String::length)`. **`flatMap(fn)`** takes a function that returns a *Stream*, then flattens the results — one input can produce zero or many outputs. Classic use: `Stream<List<X>>` → `Stream<X>` via `flatMap(List::stream)`. Or reading many files: `filenames.stream().flatMap(f -> readLines(f).stream())`. flatMap is what lets you build one big stream from many nested collections in one pipeline. Rule of thumb: use `map` for 1-to-1 transformations, `flatMap` for 1-to-many.

**Q4: When should you NOT use parallel streams?**

**A:** Several situations. **Small data** — the overhead of splitting work, coordinating threads, and merging results dominates when you only have a few hundred elements. **I/O-bound work** — parallel streams use the ForkJoinPool common pool, which is sized for CPU-bound work. Blocking on network or DB inside a parallel stream starves the pool for other work. Use `CompletableFuture` or virtual threads for I/O. **Shared mutable state** — parallel streams race if you mutate outside collectors. Any `forEach` that touches a shared variable is a bug. **Non-splittable sources** — `LinkedList` splits poorly; `HashSet` iteration order is undefined. **Order-dependent pipelines** — some operations (`findFirst`, `limit`) are more expensive in parallel because ordering must be preserved. Rule: measure first, then parallelize.

**Q5: What's the difference between `reduce` and `collect`?**

**A:** Both are terminal operations that combine stream elements, but at different levels. **`reduce`** performs a fold: repeatedly combine two elements into one via a `BinaryOperator`. Great for numeric aggregates: `stream.reduce(0, Integer::sum)` sums ints. Requires the combining function to be **associative** for parallelism. **`collect`** uses a `Collector` — a more general accumulation pattern with three parts: create a mutable container, add elements to it, and combine partial containers. Better for building complex results — lists, maps, StringBuilder joins. Rule of thumb: `reduce` for numeric folds and immutable accumulation; `collect` for building mutable containers. `Collectors.toList()`, `groupingBy`, `joining` are all built on `collect`.

**Q6: Can you reuse a Stream?**

**A:** No. A Stream is a one-time-use pipeline — once a terminal operation runs, the stream is 'consumed' and any further operation throws `IllegalStateException: stream has already been operated upon or closed`. If you need to run multiple pipelines, create a new stream each time from the source collection. This is a common trap when refactoring: extracting `Stream<X> s = list.stream()...` into a variable makes it tempting to reuse — don't. Streams are single-use by design; the alternative would be a lot of buffering.

**Q7: What are `IntStream`, `LongStream`, and `DoubleStream` for?**

**A:** They're specialized primitive streams to avoid autoboxing overhead. `Stream<Integer>` boxes every int into an Integer object — millions of allocations for millions of items. `IntStream` works with raw `int` values throughout — no boxing, no GC pressure, dramatically faster for numeric work. You get them via `.mapToInt(...)`, `.mapToLong(...)`, `IntStream.range(0, n)`, etc. They also expose numeric-specific terminals: `sum()`, `average()`, `min()`, `max()`, `summaryStatistics()`. Whenever your stream processes numbers, use the primitive variant.

## Real-world examples

- **Every REST endpoint** that transforms and returns data does `repo.findAll().stream().map(dtoMapper).toList()`.
- **Reports and dashboards** are natural `groupingBy` + `averaging` + `counting` pipelines.
- **Log analysis** — `files.stream().flatMap(readLines).filter(errorPattern).count()` is one line vs 30 lines of imperative code.
- **Spring, Jackson, Hibernate** internally use streams for pipeline transformations.
- **Every 'top N by X'** query becomes a stream one-liner.

## Advanced notes

- **Spliterators** — the underlying primitive of streams. Custom sources implement `Spliterator<T>` for parallel-friendly splitting.
- **`teeing` (Java 12+)** — a collector that feeds elements to two downstream collectors and merges the results.
- **`gathering` (Java 22+)** — advanced intermediate ops beyond what `map`/`filter` allow (custom window operations, running aggregates).
- **Stream vs Iterable** — Streams are pull-based, lazy, single-use. Iterables are re-iterable but eager.
- **Reactive Streams (Publisher/Subscriber)** — different beast, backpressure-aware, async. Similar API surface, very different semantics.
- **`toList()` (Java 16+)** — unmodifiable, faster than `Collectors.toList()`. Prefer it.

flashcards.back: Intermediate ops are lazy and return a Stream (filter, map, sorted). Terminal ops trigger execution and return a result (collect, forEach, reduce).
Enables operation fusion (single pass), short-circuiting (stop early), and infinite streams. No wasted work.
map: 1-to-1 transformation. flatMap: 1-to-many, flattens Stream-of-Streams into one.
Numeric work — avoids autoboxing overhead of Stream<Integer>.
No — throws IllegalStateException after terminal op. Create a new one from the source.

quiz.explain: collect triggers the pipeline; the others are lazy.

options: Only when a terminal op runs (lazily)
quiz.explain: Nothing runs until a terminal operation is invoked.
Short-circuit terminals stop as soon as they have an answer.

quiz.q: Best for summing an int field?
quiz.explain: mapToInt avoids autoboxing; `.sum()` is a specialized terminal.

options: Data is small or work is I/O-bound
quiz.explain: Overhead dominates on small data; parallel streams starve on I/O.

challenges.text: Sum the even numbers in [1..100] using IntStream.rangeClosed + filter + sum.
Given a list of names, count how many start with 'A' using stream + filter + count.
Given a list of orders (customer, total), print the top 3 customers by total spend.
Word-frequency counter for a text file. Use flatMap to split lines into words.
Read log lines, group by hour, count errors per hour, produce a sorted map.
Write a Collector that computes min/max/avg of a numeric field in one pass.
Benchmark: parallel vs sequential stream computing primes up to 10 million. Show the crossover point where parallel becomes faster.
